item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

A thoroughly enjoyable article.

In line with the other critiques, a think designation to the city would be a nice little detail. Maybe something like SCP-4320-1-Gamma or a set of longitude/latitude coordinates. A quick, rudimentary description of the architecture of the city might add to it as well.

Also perhaps Butler is the one who enters the building first. Perhaps he drawn to the chamber, and that could be subtly included in his dialogue. That would explain why they headed straight for the chamber. Just a thought.

item.title: Re: Thoughts
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your critique. I've been thinking of writing some sort of follow-up. Right now I'm working on another skip, but I'll be sure to revisit the idea of a follow-up to this at a later date.

item.title: Thoughts
item.content:encoded: I liked it. There’s a couple of grammatical and formatting errors that need to be fixed, but that’s minor and the overall story it’s telling is interesting. A phantom planet with some kind of near-human cultist aliens that have bred with us? It’s a nice hook. I do wish there was perhaps one more supplemental exploration log or event log to explain what happens next or even a Tale which could be interesting. Good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am definitely interested in exploring it more. I'll keep this in mind.

Also, I sort of meant for it to be presumed that 4320-Apollo includes covering up any possible exposure scenarios. The planet is hidden from scientific equipment, so implicit detections are impossible, but explicit detections can be performed through photographs. So a 4320-2 can see -1 in a photo while others can't.

But yeah, I'll work on fleshing this out over time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Thoughts
- and questions

With a whole planet to explore, I wish there was more to the exploration log (or, better yet, more logs). Rather than having the team find something huge and significant in what I read as the first building they even entered, build up to that. I want to read something in the logs to support the speculation in the findings report: have the team discover some sites they think are places of worship, or dwelling places, or workplaces. Let them find some texts and artworks that they can document. Or, at least, some suggestion of why that specific site was chosen for the first exploration would be a nice touch: is it the largest, or most intact city on the planet? The one with the most robot activity? Was the "powerful spacial-temporal anomaly" detected there before the ground exploration?

I wouldn't want to bog this down with too much pseudoscience detail, but I'm very curious about how some parts of the planet's non-detection/non-interaction work. From the fact that exploration occurred, we can see that it has some kind of gravitational field, but that it cuts off at some point before it would affect earth. Yet, it's somehow tied in orbit to earth. I'd find it worth mentioning, at least, that a goal of the exploration is to look for machinery or anomalies that could be the source of the gravitational suppression, or used in keeping the planet in orbit. Re: the non-detection effect, from my reading, I interpreted that as working on a psychic level, making the planet ignorable, rather than truly invisible. Is this how I should read it? and, if so, how does that affect scientific instruments whose path the planet crosses? Does the "ignore me" effect cover even blips in data caused by it blocking more distant objects being observed, or photographs in which it would be present (perhaps causing these "blips" to be dismissed as noise, or smudges on the lens)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I wrote the original version of this (which can still be found at scp-4320)

Noting that per the Deletion Guidelines, that page has been cleared. If you would like to archive a previous version of a page prior to a repost of a revamped version, please contact staff with regards to how best to go about doing that.

I've sent you a copy of the most recent source text for the previous version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't tell me the moon is shining; show me the glint of light on broken glass.

- Anton Chekhov


Take two.

So I wrote the original version of this a month back and now I've rewritten it thanks to several critiquers. I honestly prefer this new version over my old one.

I will answer questions regarding the story and content of the article if said questions are asked in a reply to this post.
